meta.title: Z8 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW Z8 E52 engine — S62 4.9 V8 and Alpina M62 4.8 V8. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the S62 vs Alpina choice explained. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW Z8 E52 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Z8 engine typically costs £7,000–£15,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on engine — the Alpina M62B48 sits at £7,000–£12,000, while the S62B50 can reach £8,000–£15,000. Fitting adds £910–£1,300. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Z8 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both engines are highly regarded. The S62B50 4.9-litre V8 — shared with the legendary E39 M5 — is a masterpiece of naturally aspirated engineering, producing 400 hp and 500 Nm of torque. The Alpina M62B48 is more relaxed, with 375 hp and 520 Nm torque, offering a smoother, more GT‑oriented driving experience. Both are robust engines but VANOS and timing chain maintenance are essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW Z8 E52?

acceptedAnswer.text: VANOS wear on the S62 (reduced low‑end torque, rattling), timing chain guide wear on both S62 and M62 (chain rattle, potential engine failure), oil consumption on the S62 (normal but monitor), and — unrelated to the engine — cooling system wear on all aluminium‑bodied Z8s.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW Z8 E52 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the S62 and M62 are proven, high‑quality V8 engines. The S62 is shared with the E39 M5, which has a strong reliability reputation. However, both engines require proactive maintenance — VANOS rebuilds, timing chain guide replacement, and regular oil changes are essential. A well‑maintained Z8 with documented service history can be a reliable modern classic.

mainEntity.name: Which Z8 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine to avoid — both are excellent. The S62 is the more performance‑oriented choice with the 6‑speed manual; the Alpina M62 is more relaxed with the 5‑speed automatic. Choose based on your driving preference. Budget for VANOS and timing chain maintenance regardless of which engine you choose.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my Z8 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs: rattling on start‑up (VANOS or timing chain issues), reduced low‑end torque (VANOS wear), excessive oil consumption (valve stem seals), or persistent overheating (cooling system). Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Z8 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a £100,000+ Z8, a £8,000–£15,000 S62 recon is 8–15% of the car's value — safe. The Z8 is a collector car; engine replacement costs are a fraction of the vehicle's value. Our 70% BMW threshold is almost never breached — but if you're considering a tired, high‑mileage example, assess the car's overall condition first.

mainEntity.name: How long do Z8 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S62 and M62 V8s can exceed 150,000 miles with proper servicing (regular oil changes, VANOS maintenance, timing chain inspection). The S62's oil consumption is normal but should be monitored. Many Z8s are low‑mileage collector cars, so engine life is often measured in decades rather than miles.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the standard Z8 and the Alpina Roadster V8?

acceptedAnswer.text: The standard Z8 (1999–2003) uses the S62B50 4.9-litre V8 producing 400 hp, mated to a 6-speed manual transmission. The Alpina Roadster V8 Limited Edition (2002–2003, 555 units) uses the M62B48 4.8-litre V8 producing 375 hp, mated to a 5-speed Steptronic automatic. The Alpina is more GT‑oriented, with a softer ride and conventional 20‑inch tyres replacing the Z8's run‑flats.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW Z8 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine number is stamped on the block — on the S62 it's on the front of the block near the timing cover; on the M62 it's on the left‑hand side. Tell us your variant and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW Z8 E52 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the iconic Z8 roadster — from the S62 4.9-litre V8 shared with the E39 M5 to the Alpina Roadster V8's M62 4.8-litre V8. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for BMW's aluminium-bodied modern classic. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW Z8 E52 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Z8 (E52) specifically — from the 1999 launch to the 2003 end of production.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are [PROVISIONAL] — no dedicated Engine Page exists for either S62 or M62 family in the context of the Z8. Ratings are derived from triangulated specialist sources (BMW Group Classic, Wikipedia, Hemmings, BMWBlog, owner forums). The S62B50 is a proven, high‑performance engine from BMW Motorsport, shared with the E39 M5 — it is generally reliable for a high‑output V8 but can suffer from VANOS wear, timing chain guide wear, and oil consumption as mileage accumulates. The Alpina M62B48 (coded F5 by Alpina) is a more relaxed, torquier variant with 375 hp and 520 Nm of torque.

overview.intro: The Z8 is a limited‑production roadster produced by BMW from 1999 to 2003, developed under the codename "E52". Designed by Henrik Fisker with interior by Scott Lempert, the Z8 was conceived as a modern tribute to the 1956–1959 BMW 507. The design was previewed by the Z07 concept at the 1997 Tokyo Motor Show. The Z8 featured an all‑aluminium spaceframe chassis and body panels — a first for BMW — with the engine mounted behind the front axle for 50/50 weight distribution. Under the bonnet, the standard Z8 used the S62B50 V8 from the E39 M5, producing 400 hp, mated exclusively to a 6‑speed manual transmission. In 2002, Alpina offered the Roadster V8 Limited Edition with a 375 hp 4.8‑litre M62 V8 and 5‑speed automatic.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: All‑aluminium spaceframe (first for BMW) • Predecessor: BMW 507 (spiritual predecessor) • Successor: BMW Z8 (no direct successor) • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (Z8 is 2‑door roadster only; Alpina Roadster V8 is a derivative).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The Z8 generates consistent UK enquiries [PATTERN DATA], concentrated on the S62‑powered standard Z8 as these modern classics enter collectible and investment territory.

items.quote: Most dependable: Both Z8 engines are highly regarded. The S62B50 4.9‑litre V8 — shared with the legendary E39 M5 — is a masterpiece of naturally aspirated engineering, producing 400 hp and 500 Nm of torque. It is robust for a high‑output V8 but VANOS wear, timing chain guides, and oil consumption are well‑documented at higher mileages. The Alpina M62B48 is more relaxed, with 375 hp and 520 Nm torque, offering a smoother, more GT‑oriented driving experience. Buyers should prioritise a well‑maintained example with documented service history and evidence of VANOS maintenance.

rows.commonMajorFailure: VANOS wear, timing chain guides, oil consumption
rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Engine — the E39 M5's legendary V8

economicsRule.title: The Z8 rule
economicsRule.text: A £8,000–£15,000 S62 recon against a £80,000–£250,000 Z8 sits at 3–19% of the car's value — safe. The Z8 is a collector car; engine replacement costs are a fraction of the vehicle's value. Our 70% BMW threshold is almost never breached — but if you're considering a tired, high‑mileage example, assess the car's overall condition first.

problems.title: S62 VANOS Wear
problems.rootCause: The S62's dual‑VANOS system is a known weak point — wear in the VANOS units leads to reduced low‑end torque, rough idle, and a rattling noise on start‑up. This is a well‑documented issue on the E39 M5 and Z8.
problems.recommendation: On any Z8, budget for VANOS inspection as part of routine maintenance — a £1,200–£2,500 rebuild is cheaper than the £8,000–£12,000 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare S62 engine prices for your Z8 →

problems.title: S62 Timing Chain Guide Wear
problems.rootCause: The S62's timing chain guides are made of plastic that degrades over time — leading to chain rattle and, in extreme cases, catastrophic engine failure if the chain skips.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of chain rattle, get the timing chain inspected — a £1,000–£2,200 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £8,000–£12,000 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare S62 timing chain repair quotes →

problems.title: S62 Oil Consumption
problems.rootCause: The S62 is known for moderate oil consumption — typically 1 litre per 1,000–2,000 miles — which is considered normal by BMW. However, excessive consumption can indicate valve stem seal wear or piston ring wear.
problems.recommendation: Monitor oil consumption regularly — if it exceeds 1 litre per 1,000 miles, have the valve stem seals inspected.

cta.label: Compare S62 engine prices for your Z8 →

problems.title: Alpina M62 Timing Chain Guide Wear
problems.affectedModels: Alpina Roadster V8 (2002–2003) with M62B48 engine.
problems.rootCause: The M62 V8 shares the timing chain guide weakness of the S62 — plastic guides degrade over time, leading to chain rattle and potential engine damage.
problems.recommendation: On any Alpina Z8, budget for timing chain inspection at 60k miles — £800–£1,800 now is cheaper than the £6,000–£9,000 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare M62 engine prices for your Alpina Z8 →

All costs are [BMW-QUOTE] from Table A — Engine Replacement Economics (estimated from specialist supplier data).
